key_insight: Lattice reconstruction in TaCo2Te2 reshapes electronic symmetry through orbital hybridization and suppresses a magnetic instability, favoring a nonmagnetic ground state.
engineering_relevance: Controlling structural reconstruction offers a practical route to tune magnetism and low-energy electronic behavior in layered quantum materials.
method: STM/STS mapped bias-dependent electronic contrast and local density of states.
nc-AFM resolved the distorted Te surface lattice.
ARPES and orbital-projected DFT linked reconstructed bands to anisotropic Fermi-surface behavior and weakened magnetism.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports converging STM/STS, nc-AFM, ARPES, and DFT evidence that reconstructed states produce square-like electronic symmetry and stabilize nonmagnetism.
watch_item: The contrast in STM does not directly mirror atomic structure, so interpretation depends on energy-integrated electronic states rather than topography alone.
paper_title: Lattice Reconstruction and Orbital Hybridization Suppress Magnetism in TaCo$_2$Te$_2$
